content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcer- PRAcrrcps CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 9581.1-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Aprrl22,20lO
Harry Wiggins
35 Secondo Way
Watsonville, CA 95076
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-061
Dear Mr. Wiggins
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it
renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
intercst or Govemment Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
The Pajaro Sunny Mesa Community Services District (CSD) is currently the plaintiff in
a lawsuit against the Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (Water Agency) challenging a
2OO2 fee ordinance enacted by the Water Agency as it was applied to the CSD's wells. you
have asked whether you, in your capacity as a member of the Board of Directors of the CSD,
have a conflict of interest in participating in settlement discussions regarding the litigation, if you
have properties which are affected by the fee ordinance?
CONCLUSION
As discussed below, if the decision will have a material financial affect on any of your
economic interests, absent any exceptions, you may not participate in the decision.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Cocle Sections lll000 through 91014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the GovernmentCode, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations oIthe FairPolitical practices
CommissionarecontainedinSections lttll0through ltt997of Title2of theCalifbrniaCodeof Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to 'f itle 2, Division 6 of the Califurnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 2
FACTS
fne paiero Vattev Wa 
: The Water Agency was formed to
efficiently and economically manage "*irtirg urd nffi-oter supplies tJprevent and reduce
overdraft and to provide sufficient water supplies for present and-anticipated needs for its
boundaries. The State has granted the WateiAg"n.y u source of revenue to sustain itself upon
creation. Its primary sources of income are volume tharges on agricultural wells and sales of
recycled water.
The Water Agency adopted an ordinan ce in 2Oo2 increasing the rates to $80.00 per acre
foot of water pumped from wells within the Water Agency's 110 sluare mile jurisdiction. The
reasoning behind the ordinance was to assist agricultural lands in gaining treaied water for
growing crops. The Water Agency spent some of the proceeds oflts ratJ increase to help pay for
a pipe distribution system to distribute recycled waterirom Watsonville's wastewater plant.
The Water Agency sells its treated water for agricultural irrigation. There are about 100
"tllrnouts" available for customers to attach their agricultural irrigation pipes. Based on your
belief, there are several parcels served by each turn-out. The Watir agency began delivering
treated water to customers in 2009. The tenants on one of your prope.tie. ur" ih.r" turnouts
from time to time, in order to grow crops.
Agricultural water users pay the Water Agency for the volume of water they use. There
is no stand-by, basic or access charge. The morelheyuse the more they pay. Customers are
charged by volume, acre foot or portion thereof.
The CSD was formed in 1992
with a five member board and owns *d op"-ato rffiliuter systems in Monterey county,
including one serving the unincorporated area of Pajaro and anotler serving the sunny Mesa
area' You are a member of the Board of Directors that governs the CSD. itr" CSD piovides
four thousand residents and businesses with potable ,oi., which it obtains from wells in the
CSD' The CSD's wells are subject to the water Agency's 2002 assessment. The CSD pays a
per acre foot charge for water pumped from its wells, oi do", the City of Watsonville. The
water Agency's charges are then passed to the cSD's customers.
Your InterestEi You state that you and your wife have ownership interest in two parcels
of land through a family living trust:
o d forty-acre parcgl o.f unimproved agricultural land near the coast. This property is outside
the CSD boundaries but inside the Water Agency boundaries. The pur..i is fapable of being
served by a tumout of the Water Agency's recycied water line. The Trust leases the forty
acres to a company to grow crops. In our phone conversations, you had mentioned that you
eam up to $1,250.per acre annually from the tenant leasing that iroperty from you. There are
ground wells on the parcel but they are not considered to 6e of ruffi.i.ru quality for irrigating


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 3
row crops. This may change from year to year depending on the quality of the water. The
tenants on this land have two water sources. The tenant pumps some of the water from
agricultural wells on the property and pay the assessment to the Water Agency for its use. It
also purchases some of its agricultural water from the Water Agency's recycied system.
o The second property that you have an ownership interest in is your home. It also is held in
the name of your living trust and is inside both the CSD and Water Agency boundaries.
The CSD filed a lawsuit against the Water Agency challenging the Water Agency's 2002
fee ordinance as it applied to the CSD's wells. The lawsuit alleges that the Water Agency's 2002
ordinance was constitutionally invalid and improperly approved. The CSD and the Watei
Agency are the only parties in the lawsuit. You indicate that the Water Agency has advised that
if the fee ordinance is invalidated, the resulting loss of revenue to the Water Agency will require
(or at least encourage) the Water Agency to increase its rates for properties within its
unquestioned jurisdiction, which includes the 40 acre agricultural property in which you and
your wife have an ownership interest.
The CSD recently entered into settlement discussions with the Water Agency. you were
asked by the attorneys for the CSD not to participate in these settlement discussions due to your
above-mentioned properties, especially the agricultural property which you lease. According to
the attomeys for the CSD, since you have an economic interest involved in the fee ordinanci,
you could have a conflict of interest if you participate in any decision involving possible
settlement of the lawsuit.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflicrof-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section g7100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The genLral rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one orynor" of his or her
financial interests.
Srrp 1: Axr You A puBt,rc oFFICTAL?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. A public official
includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency .


File No. 4-10-061
Page No. 4
." (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a)(1).) As an appointed member of the Board of Directors of
the CSD, you are a public official under the Act. (Section g204g.)
STTP 2: WN,T, YOU BE NIAKING, PARTICIPATING IN MAKING oR INFLUENCING A GoVERNMENTAL
nuctsroN?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (See Regulation 18702.1.) Apublic official "participates in a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without sign-ificant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702 .2.) Apublic official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmentil decision that is before
his or her agency when, for the purposes of influencing the decision, the official contacts or
appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant
of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) A public official is attempting io ur" his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision that is before un og.r"!, other than the
official's agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official o.t, o. purports to
act, on behalf of, or as the representative of his or her agency. (Regulation taZOZ.j6).)2
The settlement discussions between the Water Agency and the CSD regarding the fee
ordinance will require the board members of both agencies to make, participatE in miking, or to
use their official positions to influence the making of a govemmentaldecision as they nelotiate
towards a settlement agreement.
STTT 3: Do YoU HAvE A PoTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING EC6NSMIC INTERE5T?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87I03 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87 103(a); Regulation I g703. I (a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section g7103(d); Regulation tszo].tiu).)
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*8720}filers" include mayors and city
councilmembers) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision considered in the closed session of a public
meeting, then he or she must verbally recuse himself or herself from the decision. The public identification may be
made orally during the open session before the body goes into closed session and shall Ue limiteO to a declaration
that his or her recusal is because of a contlict of interest under Government Code Section g7 100. The declaration
shall be made part of the official public record. The public ofllcial shall not be present when the decision is
considered in closed session or knowingly obtain or review a recording or ony oih., non-public intbrmation
regarding the governmental decision


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 5
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
The economic interests you have described are the two parcels of land in which you own
through a family trust.
As to your economic interest in the real property, the concluding paragraph of Section
87103 provides:
"For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest
means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official,
the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater."
We presume, for purposes of this letter, that you and your wife have at least a ten percent
interest in the family trust.
Regulation 18234 provides, in pertinent part, as follows:
"(a) An official has an economic interest in the pro rata
share of the interests in real property, sources of income, and
investments of a trust in which the official has a direct, indirect, or
beneficial interest of 10 percent or greater.
"(b) For purposes of this section, the interests of the official
include those of the official, spouse, and dependent children
regarding interests in real property and investments and those of
the official and spouse regarding sources of income.
o


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 6
"(c) For purposes of determining whether an official has an
economic interest in interests in real property sources of income,
and investments of a trust, the official has a direct, indirect, or
beneficial interest in a trust if the official is:
"(l) A trustor and:
"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;
"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or
principal of the trust, or retained any reversionary or remainder
interest; or
"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment,
including but not limited to the power to ciange the irustee, or rhe
power to amend, alter or designate, either alone or in conjunction
with anyone else, the person or persons who shall possess or enjoy
the trust property or income."
Assuming you have the powers set forth above, and assuming your interest (including
your community property interest) in each property based on your pro rata share of the trust is
valued at $2,000 or more, you have an economic interest in both of these properties.
In addition, you also have an interest in the tenants of the 4}-acre property as a source of
income (Section 87103(c)), assuming that your share of the rents amountJ to $SOo or more
within the 12 month period prior to any decision.
Finally, we note that every public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances. (Section 87103)
Srrp 4: Is rHe ECoNoMIC INTEREsT DTRECTLy oR TNDTRECTLy rNvoLvED IN THE
GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?
Step 4 requires a determination of whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in the govemmental decision the public official will be making. (Regulation
18700(bx4).)
Real Property: According to Regulation l8704.2(a),the real property in which you
have an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if, o*org othei things:
o The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which
is the subject of the governmental decision.


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 7
o The govemmental decision involves . . . inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county,
district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official has
an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.
o The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or
fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has an interest.
o The decision involves constntction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storrn
drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or improved services.
The circumstances here are unusual, but it seems that the settlement process you describe
does "involve the imposition, repeal or modification of . . . taxes or fees assessed or imposed on
the real property in which the official has an interest," because the subject matter of the lawsuit is
a fee ordinance in one area which, if invalidated, would predictably result in an increase in the
rates imposed on the adjacent area that includes your agricultural property. In essence, these two
decisions are inextricably linked together.
Construing the Act, as we must, in a manner that furthers the fundamental concern to
prevent conflicts of interest3 we conclude that your agricultural property is directly involved in
any decision affecting the outcome of that litigation.a The residential real property you described
is not directly involved in any such decision, and it is therefore indirectly involved. (Regulation
r8704.)
Business interests (sources of income that are business entities): Regulation
18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly involvedln a decision
before the official's agency when that business entity or source of income, either directly or by
an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject ofa
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
I Section 81003 provides that: "This title should be liberally construed to accomplish its purposes.', The
Act's fundamental purposes are set out at Section 8lOO2, subdivision (c) of which providis: "Assets and income of
public otficials which may be materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the offlcials should be disqualified fiom acting in order that contlicts of interest may be iuoiA.a.,'
'Even if we concluded that the agricultural property were "indirectly involved" in the governmental
decision we would arrive at the same conclusions in steps 5 and 6 (as discussed below) because the lawsuit would
have an apparent material impact on the water rates tbr the agricultural property. (See, Regulation 1g705.2(b).)


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 8
The tenants that pay you to rent the agricultural property) are neither "applicants,' in these
proceedings, nor would they be considered the subject of the decisions in question. They are
therefore only indirectly involved in those decisions.
Srrp 5: MarnnIar,ITY STANDARD.
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is materiaL (Regulation
18700(a).)
When real property (other than leasehold interests) is directly involved in a govemmental
decision, "the financial effect of a govemmental decision on the reai property is preiumed to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property." (Regulation
t87}s.2)
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in a decision is presumed to be not material, but Reguiation 18705.2 (bxl) provides for
rebuttal of that presumption also:
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effict, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest,
which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has a interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
Nothing in the facts you provided suggest that the consequences of the settlement
decision will affect your residence. In fact, the attorneys representing the CSD in the litigation --
who first raised the potential conflict of interest -- stated that only yoi, hor" a conflict of interest
in the decision (not the other Board members). Presumably this wis not based on your residence
in the CSD (as all members have residences in the CSD) birt due ro your ownershii of


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 9
agricultural property outside the CSD, but in the jurisdiction of the Water Agency that benefits
from the CSD's payment of fees.
Business Interests: with respect to your sources of rental income:
"[T]he financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for i
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of g20,000 or more.,,
Personal Financial Effects: Effects on an official's personal finances are material as
stated in Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $ 250 in any l2-month period. when
determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered.',
Since the effects at issue will be on your real property, we do not further analyzeeffects
on personal finances.
SrBp 6: Is tr RnlsoNABLy FoREsEEABLE THAT THE DECrsIoNs wrLL AFFECT youR
ECONOMIC INTERESTS MATERIALLY.
The financial effects of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" when the
effects are "substantially likely" to occur. Whether the financial consequences of a
govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision ii made depends on the
facts and circumstances pertaining at the time. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Tltorner
(1975) I FPPC ops. 198.)


File No. A-10-061
Page No. 10
Agricultural Property: We believe it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions on the
settlement of the lawsuit will have some financial effect on the agricultural property, and any
such effect would presumptively be "material."
Residential Property: On the other hand, we do not find that it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions relating to settlement of the lawsuit would have any financial effect on your
residential property.
Tenants who are Sources of Income: We also find that it is reasonably foreseeable that
these decisions will have some financial effect on the tenants farming this property. We do not
have sufficient information to calculate whether the foreseeable effect would be "material," and
must leave that determination to you.
Srpps 7: Punlrc GENERALLy ExcEprroN
Even if a governmental decision is determined to have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on a public official's economic interests, the "public official does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decision if the official can establish that the
governmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests in a manner [that] is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally . . .."
(Regulation 18707(a).) Due to the size of your agricultural property, it is unlikely that there are
enough property owners similarly situated (owning A}-acre parcels) to constitute a significant
segment.
There is a special rule that applies to rates, assessments, and other similar decisions. The
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally in certain instances, including when the decision is made
by the goveming board of a water, irrigation, or similar district to establish or adjust assessments,
taxes, fees, charges, or rates, among other decisions. The increase or decrease must be applied
proportionally on the officials' interests as well as at least ten percent of the property owners or
those receiving services from the agency. (Regulation 18707.2(c).) However, this exception
would not apply to the settlement decisions you described. While we have concluded that the
settlement decision is inextricably linked to a ratemaking proceeding in a different jurisdiction,
this does not transform the settlement decision itself into a ratemaking decision.
Finally, please note, for decisions that affect a business entity in which a public official
has an economic interest (such as your tenant farmers), the exception only applies if the decision
affects either 2,000 businesses or twenty-five percent of all business entities in the jurisdiction in
substantially the same manner. Further, segment may not be composed of a single industry,
trade, or profession. Again, it seems highly unlikely this exception would apply to your tenants.


File No. 4-10-061
Page No. 11
Srnp 8: Lrcarly Rrqurnro paRrrcrplrron
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "legally required participation,,
exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we have not provided an analysis involving
this step.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Hayat
Intern, Legal Division
rhjgl

economic interests, absent any exceptions, you may not participate in the decision.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflicrof-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section g7100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The genLral rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one orynor" of his or her
financial interests.
Srrp 1: Axr You A puBt,rc oFFICTAL?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. A public official
includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency .
." (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a)(1).) As an appointed member of the Board of Directors of
the CSD, you are a public official under the Act. (Section g204g.)
STTP 2: WN,T, YOU BE NIAKING, PARTICIPATING IN MAKING oR INFLUENCING A GoVERNMENTAL
nuctsroN?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (See Regulation 18702.1.) Apublic official "participates in a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without sign-ificant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702 .2.) Apublic official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmentil decision that is before
his or her agency when, for the purposes of influencing the decision, the official contacts or
appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant
of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) A public official is attempting io ur" his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision that is before un og.r"!, other than the
official's agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official o.t, o. purports to
act, on behalf of, or as the representative of his or her agency. (Regulation taZOZ.j6).)2
The settlement discussions between the Water Agency and the CSD regarding the fee
ordinance will require the board members of both agencies to make, participatE in miking, or to
use their official positions to influence the making of a govemmentaldecision as they nelotiate
towards a settlement agreement.
STTT 3: Do YoU HAvE A PoTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING EC6NSMIC INTERE5T?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87I03 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87 103(a); Regulation I g703. I (a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section g7103(d); Regulation tszo].tiu).)
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*8720}filers" include mayors and city
councilmembers) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision considered in the closed session of a public
meeting, then he or she must verbally recuse himself or herself from the decision. The public identification may be
made orally during the open session before the body goes into closed session and shall Ue limiteO to a declaration
that his or her recusal is because of a contlict of interest under Government Code Section g7 100. The declaration
shall be made part of the official public record. The public ofllcial shall not be present when the decision is
considered in closed session or knowingly obtain or review a recording or ony oih., non-public intbrmation
regarding the governmental decision
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
The economic interests you have described are the two parcels of land in which you own
through a family trust.
As to your economic interest in the real property, the concluding paragraph of Section
87103 provides:
"For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest
means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official,
the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater."
We presume, for purposes of this letter, that you and your wife have at least a ten percent
interest in the family trust.
Regulation 18234 provides, in pertinent part, as follows:
"(a) An official has an economic interest in the pro rata
share of the interests in real property, sources of income, and
investments of a trust in which the official has a direct, indirect, or
beneficial interest of 10 percent or greater.
"(b) For purposes of this section, the interests of the official
include those of the official, spouse, and dependent children
regarding interests in real property and investments and those of
the official and spouse regarding sources of income.
o
"(c) For purposes of determining whether an official has an
economic interest in interests in real property sources of income,
and investments of a trust, the official has a direct, indirect, or
beneficial interest in a trust if the official is:
"(l) A trustor and:
"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;
"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or
principal of the trust, or retained any reversionary or remainder
interest; or
"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment,
including but not limited to the power to ciange the irustee, or rhe
power to amend, alter or designate, either alone or in conjunction
with anyone else, the person or persons who shall possess or enjoy
the trust property or income."
Assuming you have the powers set forth above, and assuming your interest (including
your community property interest) in each property based on your pro rata share of the trust is
valued at $2,000 or more, you have an economic interest in both of these properties.
In addition, you also have an interest in the tenants of the 4}-acre property as a source of
income (Section 87103(c)), assuming that your share of the rents amountJ to $SOo or more
within the 12 month period prior to any decision.
Finally, we note that every public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances. (Section 87103)
Srrp 4: Is rHe ECoNoMIC INTEREsT DTRECTLy oR TNDTRECTLy rNvoLvED IN THE
GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?
Step 4 requires a determination of whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in the govemmental decision the public official will be making. (Regulation
18700(bx4).)
Real Property: According to Regulation l8704.2(a),the real property in which you
have an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if, o*org othei things:
o The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which
is the subject of the governmental decision.
o The govemmental decision involves . . . inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county,
district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official has
an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.
o The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or
fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has an interest.
o The decision involves constntction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storrn
drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or improved services.
The circumstances here are unusual, but it seems that the settlement process you describe
does "involve the imposition, repeal or modification of . . . taxes or fees assessed or imposed on
the real property in which the official has an interest," because the subject matter of the lawsuit is
a fee ordinance in one area which, if invalidated, would predictably result in an increase in the
rates imposed on the adjacent area that includes your agricultural property. In essence, these two
decisions are inextricably linked together.
Construing the Act, as we must, in a manner that furthers the fundamental concern to
prevent conflicts of interest3 we conclude that your agricultural property is directly involved in
any decision affecting the outcome of that litigation.a The residential real property you described
is not directly involved in any such decision, and it is therefore indirectly involved. (Regulation
r8704.)
Business interests (sources of income that are business entities): Regulation
18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly involvedln a decision
before the official's agency when that business entity or source of income, either directly or by
an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject ofa
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
I Section 81003 provides that: "This title should be liberally construed to accomplish its purposes.', The
Act's fundamental purposes are set out at Section 8lOO2, subdivision (c) of which providis: "Assets and income of
public otficials which may be materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the offlcials should be disqualified fiom acting in order that contlicts of interest may be iuoiA.a.,'
'Even if we concluded that the agricultural property were "indirectly involved" in the governmental
decision we would arrive at the same conclusions in steps 5 and 6 (as discussed below) because the lawsuit would
have an apparent material impact on the water rates tbr the agricultural property. (See, Regulation 1g705.2(b).)
The tenants that pay you to rent the agricultural property) are neither "applicants,' in these
proceedings, nor would they be considered the subject of the decisions in question. They are
therefore only indirectly involved in those decisions.
Srrp 5: MarnnIar,ITY STANDARD.
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is materiaL (Regulation
18700(a).)
When real property (other than leasehold interests) is directly involved in a govemmental
decision, "the financial effect of a govemmental decision on the reai property is preiumed to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property." (Regulation
t87}s.2)
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in a decision is presumed to be not material, but Reguiation 18705.2 (bxl) provides for
rebuttal of that presumption also:
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effict, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest,
which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has a interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
Nothing in the facts you provided suggest that the consequences of the settlement
decision will affect your residence. In fact, the attorneys representing the CSD in the litigation --
who first raised the potential conflict of interest -- stated that only yoi, hor" a conflict of interest
in the decision (not the other Board members). Presumably this wis not based on your residence
in the CSD (as all members have residences in the CSD) birt due ro your ownershii of
agricultural property outside the CSD, but in the jurisdiction of the Water Agency that benefits
from the CSD's payment of fees.
Business Interests: with respect to your sources of rental income:
"[T]he financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for i
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of g20,000 or more.,,
Personal Financial Effects: Effects on an official's personal finances are material as
stated in Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $ 250 in any l2-month period. when
determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered.',
Since the effects at issue will be on your real property, we do not further analyzeeffects
on personal finances.
SrBp 6: Is tr RnlsoNABLy FoREsEEABLE THAT THE DECrsIoNs wrLL AFFECT youR
ECONOMIC INTERESTS MATERIALLY.
The financial effects of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" when the
effects are "substantially likely" to occur. Whether the financial consequences of a
govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision ii made depends on the
facts and circumstances pertaining at the time. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Tltorner
(1975) I FPPC ops. 198.)
Agricultural Property: We believe it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions on the
settlement of the lawsuit will have some financial effect on the agricultural property, and any
such effect would presumptively be "material."
Residential Property: On the other hand, we do not find that it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions relating to settlement of the lawsuit would have any financial effect on your
residential property.
Tenants who are Sources of Income: We also find that it is reasonably foreseeable that
these decisions will have some financial effect on the tenants farming this property. We do not
have sufficient information to calculate whether the foreseeable effect would be "material," and
must leave that determination to you.
Srpps 7: Punlrc GENERALLy ExcEprroN
Even if a governmental decision is determined to have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on a public official's economic interests, the "public official does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decision if the official can establish that the
governmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests in a manner [that] is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally . . .."
(Regulation 18707(a).) Due to the size of your agricultural property, it is unlikely that there are
enough property owners similarly situated (owning A}-acre parcels) to constitute a significant
segment.
There is a special rule that applies to rates, assessments, and other similar decisions. The
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally in certain instances, including when the decision is made
by the goveming board of a water, irrigation, or similar district to establish or adjust assessments,
taxes, fees, charges, or rates, among other decisions. The increase or decrease must be applied
proportionally on the officials' interests as well as at least ten percent of the property owners or
those receiving services from the agency. (Regulation 18707.2(c).) However, this exception
would not apply to the settlement decisions you described. While we have concluded that the
settlement decision is inextricably linked to a ratemaking proceeding in a different jurisdiction,
this does not transform the settlement decision itself into a ratemaking decision.
Finally, please note, for decisions that affect a business entity in which a public official
has an economic interest (such as your tenant farmers), the exception only applies if the decision
affects either 2,000 businesses or twenty-five percent of all business entities in the jurisdiction in
substantially the same manner. Further, segment may not be composed of a single industry,
trade, or profession. Again, it seems highly unlikely this exception would apply to your tenants.
Srnp 8: Lrcarly Rrqurnro paRrrcrplrron
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "legally required participation,,
exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we have not provided an analysis involving
this step.

QUESTION: The Pajaro Sunny Mesa Community Services District (CSD) is currently the plaintiff in
a lawsuit against the Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (Water Agency) challenging a
2OO2 fee ordinance enacted by the Water Agency as it was applied to the CSD's wells. you
have asked whether you, in your capacity as a member of the Board of Directors of the CSD,
have a conflict of interest in participating in settlement discussions regarding the litigation, if you
have properties which are affected by the fee ordinance?

CONCLUSION: As discussed below, if the decision will have a material financial affect on any of your
economic interests, absent any exceptions, you may not participate in the decision.

CA 9581.1-2329 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 Aprrl22,20lO Harry Wiggins 35 Secondo Way Watsonville, CA 95076 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-061 Dear Mr. Wiggins This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of intercst or Govemment Code Section 1090. QUESTION The Pajaro Sunny Mesa Community Services District (CSD) is currently the plaintiff in a lawsuit against the Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (Water Agency) challenging a 2OO2 fee ordinance enacted by the Water Agency as it was applied to the CSD's wells. you have asked whether you, in your capacity as a member of the Board of Directors of the CSD, have a conflict of interest in participating in settlement discussions regarding the litigation, if you have properties which are affected by the fee ordinance? CONCLUSION As discussed below, if the decision will have a material financial affect on any of your economic interests, absent any exceptions, you may not participate in the decision. I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Cocle Sections lll000 through 91014. All statutory ref'erences are to the GovernmentCode, unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory ref'erences are to 'f itle 2, Division 6 of the Califurnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-061 Page No. 2 FACTS fne paiero Vattev Wa : The Water Agency was formed to efficiently and economically manage "*irtirg urd nffi-oter supplies tJprevent and reduce overdraft and to provide sufficient water supplies for present and-anticipated needs for its boundaries. The State has granted the WateiAg"n.y u source of revenue to sustain itself upon creation. Its primary sources of income are volume tharges on agricultural wells and sales of recycled water. The Water Agency adopted an ordinan ce in 2Oo2 increasing the rates to $80.00 per acre foot of water pumped from wells within the Water Agency's 110 sluare mile jurisdiction. The reasoning behind the ordinance was to assist agricultural lands in gaining treaied water for growing crops. The Water Agency spent some of the proceeds oflts ratJ increase to help pay for a pipe distribution system to distribute recycled waterirom Watsonville's wastewater plant. The Water Agency sells its treated water for agricultural irrigation. There are about 100 "tllrnouts" available for customers to attach their agricultural irrigation pipes. Based on your belief, there are several parcels served by each turn-out. The Watir agency began delivering treated water to customers in 2009. The tenants on one of your prope.tie. ur"
